headline: Supermicro Shares Slide as Company Launches $7 Billion Fundraising to Support AI Demand (SMCI)
summary: Super Micro Computer Inc (NASDAQ:SMCI) shares fell roughly 9% in premarket trading on Wednesday after the company unveiled plans for a major capital raise aimed at funding its rapidly expanding artificial intelligence server business. The fundraising package totals approximately $7 billion and is intended to provide the company with the resources needed to address a growing order backlog linked to demand for AI infrastructure.

content.title: Supermicro Shares Slide as Company Launches $7 Billion Fundraising to Support AI Demand (SMCI)
content.summary: Super Micro Computer Inc (NASDAQ:SMCI) shares fell roughly 9% in premarket trading on Wednesday after the company unveiled plans for a major capital raise aimed at funding its rapidly expanding artificial intelligence server business. The fundraising package totals approximately $7 billion and is intended to provide the company with the resources needed to address a growing order backlog linked to demand for AI infrastructure.

thumbnail.caption: chart arrow down NEW SIZE ©Gerd Altmann

headline: More drops for tech stocks weigh on Wall Street
summary: Technology stocks are continuing to fall on Wednesday, and the S&P 500 index is on track for its first back-to-back drop in three weeks.  Super Micro Computer, which sells AI servers, tumbled 13.5% after saying it plans to raise $7 billion in cash by selling shares of stock and convertible preferred stock.  Such moves raise the most money for companies when their stock prices are high, and they can dilute the ownership stakes of existing shareholders.

content.title: More drops for tech stocks weigh on Wall Street
content.summary: Technology stocks are continuing to fall on Wednesday, and the S&P 500 index is on track for its first back-to-back drop in three weeks.  Super Micro Computer, which sells AI servers, tumbled 13.5% after saying it plans to raise $7 billion in cash by selling shares of stock and convertible preferred stock.  Such moves raise the most money for companies when their stock prices are high, and they can dilute the ownership stakes of existing shareholders.

thumbnail.caption: Options traders Steven Rodriguez, left, and Marty Handler work on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange, Wednesday, June 3, 2026. (AP Photo/Richard Drew)
